                       1/ War veterans are defined by the dates of their service in the U. S. Armed Forces as follows: Vietnam E r a — s e r v e d after
                    August 4, 1964; Korean Conflict—served at any time between June 27, 1950 and January 31, 1955; World War I I — s e r v e d at any time from
                    September 16, 1940 to July 25, 1947$ World War I — s e r v e d at any time between April 6, 1917 and November 11, 1918. A Post-Korean-
                    Peace time veteran served in the Armed Forces between February 1, 1955 and August 4, 1964, inclusive. A person who served in more than
                    one period is classified according to his latest period of service. A Nonveteran never served in the Armed Forces, or served only in
                    peacetime prior to June 27, 1950.

                       2/    Percent not shown where base is less than 75,000.

                    made up 32 percent of the total number of nonsupervisory employees in establishments reporting hours and earnings data.

                         1
                            The private economy includes government enterprises which cover over half their current operating expenses by the sale of goods and services, e.g.. Post Office, T V A , State liquor stores, etc. This comprises about
                   10 percent of total civilian government employment.
                         2
                            Man-hours refer to hours paid for all persons—wage and salaried workers, self-employed, unpaid family workers and private household workers—and are based largely on establishment data.
                         3
                            Wages and salaries of employees plus employers' contributions for social insurance and private benefit plans. Also includes an estimate of wages, salaries, and supplementary payments for the self-emplov ed.
                         4
                            Compensation per man-hour adjusted for changes in the Consumer Price Index.
                         5
                            IMonlabor payments include profits, depreciation, interest, rental income, and indirect taxes.
                         6
                            Percent change computed from original data.
                         7
                           Current quarter divided by comparable quarter a year ago.
                         p=preliminary.

                         SOURCE: Output data from the Office of Business Economic, U.S. Department of Commerce. Man-hours and compensation of all persons from the Bureau of Labor Statistics, Office of Productivity and Technology.
                   See BLS Handbook of Methods for Surveys and Studies—Chapter 22. Output Per Man-Hour Measures, Private Economy.

                         Computed by dividing the number of vacancies by the sum of employment plus vacancies and multiplying that quotient by 100.
                      2 Long-term job vacancies are those vacancies that have remained unfilled for 30 days or more. The long-term job vacancy rate is computed by dividing the number of long-term vacancies by the sum of employ-
                    it plus all job vacancies and multiplying that quotient by 100.
                      p= preliminary.

                                                                 Seasonal                 Adjustments

                        Many economic statistics reflect a regularly recurring sea-        approximation based on past experience. Seasonally adjusted
                  sonal movement which can be estimated on the basis of past               estimates have a broader margin of possible error than the
                  experience. By eliminating that part of the change which can             original data on which they are based, since they are subject
                  be ascribed to usual seasonal variation, it is possible t o ob-          not only t o sampling and other errors but, in addition, are
                  serve the cyclical and other nonseasonal movements in the                affected by the uncertainties of the seasonal adjustment proc-
                  series. However, in evaluating deviations f r o m the seasonal           ess itself. Seasonally adjusted series for selected labor force
                  p a t t e r n — t h a t is, changes in a seasonally adjusted series—     and establishment data are published regularly in     Employment
                  it is important t o note that seasonal adjustment is merely an           and Earnings.

http://fraser.stlouisfed.org/
Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis
                     The seasonal adjustment method used for these series               The revised seasonally adjusted series for the establishment
                 is an adaptation of the standard ratio-to-moving average            data reflect experience through February 1970. Seasonal factors

                      As discussed in the Technical Note, the Bureau periodically        Beginning with the June 1970 and subsequent issues of
                  adjusts the industry employment series to a recent benchmark       Employment and Earnings, the national data in Section B,
                  to improve their accuracy. These adjustments may also affect       C, and D supersede those published in previous issues, as well
                  the hours, earnings, job vacancy, and labor turnover series be-    as those appearing in the Handbook of Labor Statistics, 1970.
                  cause employment levels are used as weights. Industry data for     Comparable data will be published in Employment and Earn-
                  all national series shown in this report have been adjusted to     ings, United States, 1909-70, BLS Bulletin 1312-7.
                  March 1969 benchmarks. Data from April 1969 forward are
                  subject to revision at the time of the next benchmark.
